---
title: 'Three tips for creating an effective and intriguing logline or elevator pitch'
date: '2014-03-31T05:28:00.0010000+00:00'
draft: true
blogger_blog: 'Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller'
blogger_status: 'LIVE'
blogger_post_id: '8114050503241351547'
blogger_filename: '/2014/03/three-tips-for-creating-effective-and_30.html'
blogger_source: 'takeout-20260223T235336Z-3-001.zip:Takeout/Blogger/Blogs/Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller/feed.atom'
categories:
  - 'Writing Practice'
  - 'Lessons in Fiction'
---

<h3 style="text-align: center;"><br />03/31/2014 The Perfect Elevator Pitch (Also Called a Logline)</h3><br /><br /><br />I came across this article from "<a href="http://thewritepractice.com/great-logline/?utm_source=feedburner&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=Feed%3A+TheWritePractice+%28The+Write+Practice%29" target="_blank">The Write Practice</a>" about creating a perfect logline or elevator pitch. The last place I saw an explanation this simple yet thorough was "<a href="http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/0470530707/ref=as_li_ss_tl?ie=UTF8&amp;camp=1789&amp;creative=390957&amp;creativeASIN=0470530707&amp;linkCode=as2&amp;tag=your0f4d-20">Writing Fiction For Dummies</a><img alt="" border="0" src="http://ir-na.amazon-adsystem.com/e/ir?t=your0f4d-20&amp;l=as2&amp;o=1&amp;a=0470530707" height="1" style="border: none !important; margin: 0px !important;" width="1" />", (Affiliate Link). I wanted to archive this information for my later reference. The following is on excerpt from the article.<br /><br /><br /><br />****<br /><br /><blockquote class="tr_bq" style="border: 0px; font-family: Merriweather; font-size: 16px; line-height: 24px; margin-bottom: 25px; outline: 0px; padding: 0px; vertical-align: baseline;"><br /><a href="http://thewritepractice.com/great-logline/?utm_source=feedburner&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=Feed%3A+TheWritePractice+%28The+Write+Practice%29" target="_blank">The Nitty-Gritty</a><br /><br /><b>Final tips for creating an effective and intriguing logline:</b><br /><br /><ol style="border: 0px; font-family: Merriweather; font-size: 16px; line-height: 24px; margin: 0px 0px 25px 30px; outline: 0px; padding: 0px; vertical-align: baseline;"><br /><li style="border: 0px; font-size: 16px; margin: 0px; outline: 0px; padding: 0px; vertical-align: baseline;">Start with your protagonist. Who or what leads the action of the story? In Star Wars, that’s obvious: Luke Skywalker. But what about an ensemble film like Pulp Fiction?&nbsp;IMDB has a great logline for Tarantino’s film: “The lives of two mob hit men, a boxer, a gangster’s wife, and a pair of diner bandits intertwine in four tales of violence and redemption.”</li><br /></ol><br /><ol style="border: 0px; font-family: Merriweather; font-size: 16px; line-height: 24px; margin: 0px 0px 25px 30px; outline: 0px; padding: 0px; vertical-align: baseline;"><br /><li style="border: 0px; font-size: 16px; margin: 0px; outline: 0px; padding: 0px; vertical-align: baseline;">With a logline, you really need to think about your verb. Steer clear of the infinitive “to be.” Look at the Lion King example above: tricked—flees—abandons. All verbs that get you thinking more about the how and the why within the main plot.</li><br /></ol><br /><ol style="border: 0px; font-family: Merriweather; font-size: 16px; line-height: 24px; margin: 0px 0px 25px 30px; outline: 0px; padding: 0px; vertical-align: baseline;"><br /><li style="border: 0px; font-size: 16px; margin: 0px; outline: 0px; padding: 0px; vertical-align: baseline;">Finally, that brings us to the basic “what happens” of the plot. What happens in Star Wars? “Luke Skywalker, a spirited farm boy, joins rebel forces to save Princess Leia from the evil Darth Vader, and the galaxy from the Empire’s planet-destroying Death Star.”</li><br /></ol><br />Simplified further: “A spirited farm boy joins rebel forces to save a princess from evil forces, and the galaxy from a planet-destroying starship.” &nbsp;</blockquote><br /><blockquote class="tr_bq" style="border: 0px; font-family: Merriweather; font-size: 16px; line-height: 24px; margin-bottom: 25px; outline: 0px; padding: 0px; vertical-align: baseline;"><br /><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman'; font-size: small; line-height: normal;">To read the FULL article (</span><a href="http://thewritepractice.com/great-logline/?utm_source=feedburner&amp;utm_medium=feed&amp;utm_campaign=Feed%3A+TheWritePractice+%28The+Write+Practice%29" style="font-family: 'Times New Roman'; font-size: medium; line-height: normal;" target="_blank">Click Here</a><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman'; font-size: small; line-height: normal;">)</span></blockquote><br />****<br /><br /><br /><br />This Longline or Elevator Pitch can be used in nearly any arena where you might have a product, service, idea, book, or story you want to convey to other people in 30 seconds or less.<br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><blockquote class="tr_bq"><br />"I'm Joe. I run Mama Mia's Pizza in downtown Brooklyn, NY. We serve fresh - old world style- pizza daily, just like Mama used to make."&nbsp;</blockquote><br /><br /><br />This could become the way this imaginary Joe would explain his business to anyone he meets.<br /><br /><br /><br /><br />I subscribed to "The Write Practice" and you should too if you want other great tips. I've really enjoyed their articles so far.<br /><br /><br /><br />Live Long And Prosper, Darrell<br /><br /><br /><br /><h3><br />Resource:&nbsp;</h3><br /><br /><br />For more information on writing fiction, this is my favorite book on the subject to date.<br /><br /><h4><br /><br /><a href="http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/0470530707/ref=as_li_ss_tl?ie=UTF8&amp;camp=1789&amp;creative=390957&amp;creativeASIN=0470530707&amp;linkCode=as2&amp;tag=your0f4d-20">Writing Fiction For Dummies</a><img alt="" border="0" src="http://ir-na.amazon-adsystem.com/e/ir?t=your0f4d-20&amp;l=as2&amp;o=1&amp;a=0470530707" height="1" style="border: none !important; margin: 0px !important;" width="1" />", (Affiliate Link).&nbsp;</h4><br /><br /><br /><blockquote class="tr_bq"><br /><b>A complete guide to writing and selling your novel</b>&nbsp;</blockquote><br /><blockquote class="tr_bq"><br /><br /><b>So you want to write a novel?</b> Great! That’s a worthy goal, no matter what your reason. But don’t settle for just writing a novel. Aim high. Write a novel that you intend to sell to a publisher. Writing Fiction for Dummies is a complete guide designed to coach you every step along the path from beginning writer to royalty-earning author. Here are some things you’ll learn in Writing Fiction for Dummies:&nbsp;</blockquote><br /><blockquote class="tr_bq"><br /><b>Strategic Planning: </b>Pinpoint where you are on the roadmap to publication; discover what every reader desperately wants from a story; home in on a marketable category; choose from among the four most common creative styles; and learn the self-management methods of professional writers.&nbsp;</blockquote><br /><blockquote class="tr_bq"><br /><b>Writing Powerful Fiction:</b> Construct a story world that rings true; create believable, unpredictable characters; build a strong plot with all six layers of complexity of a modern novel; and infuse it all with a strong theme.&nbsp;</blockquote><br /><blockquote class="tr_bq"><br /><b>Self-Editing Your Novel:</b> Psychoanalyze your characters to bring them fully to life; edit your story structure from the top down; fix broken scenes; and polish your action and dialogue.&nbsp;</blockquote><br /><blockquote class="tr_bq"><br /><b>Finding An Agent and Getting Published</b>: Write a query letter, a synopsis, and a proposal; pitch your work to agents and editors without fear.&nbsp;</blockquote><br /><blockquote class="tr_bq"><br /><b>Writing Fiction For Dummies takes you from being a writer to being an author</b>. It can happen—if you have the talent and persistence to do what you need to do.&nbsp;</blockquote><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br />&nbsp;******<br /><br /><br /><br />Thank you for reading. I hope you enjoyed this. I'd love to make a community with this project. If you would like to add, comment on, critique, or otherwise participate... that would be great!<br /><br /><br /><br />Please include any information about yourself, your blog/wesbite/amazon author page that you want to share with the community also.<br /><br /><br /><br /><i>* I may include "Affiliate Links" which means I earn a commission when you buy products I link to. To see my full about page and disclosures:&nbsp;<a href="http://authorfun.blogspot.com/p/what-is-this.html" target="_blank">Click Here</a></i><br /><br /><br /><br /><a href="http://authorfun.blogspot.com/" target="_blank">It's Author Fun</a>&nbsp;By&nbsp;<a href="https://plus.google.com/+DarrellWolfe/posts" target="_blank">Darrell Wolfe</a><br /><br /><br /><br />To Subscribe to the&nbsp;<a href="http://feeds.feedburner.com/AuthorFun" target="_blank">RSS: Click Here</a><br /><br /><br /><br />For Non-Fiction writing by&nbsp;<a href="http://www.darrellwolfe.com/" target="_blank">Darrell G. Wolfe: Click Here</a><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><div style="clear: both; float: left; padding-bottom: 10px; text-align: right;"><br /><br /></div><br />
